    Sprint 0 – Problem Framing, Infrastructure Setup & RAG Foundations

    📄

    Embedding models & vector DB integration

    Lesson 4

    Introduction to Embeddings and Vector Databases


    Vector databases are data management systems designed to store and query high-dimensional vectors (embeddings) produced by machine learning models. An embedding is a numeric representation of data (text, images, etc.) in a high-dimensional space, capturing the data’s meaning or features in a vector form. These vectors are essentially compressed representations of the original data learned by models (for tasks like language understanding, image recognition, etc.), such that similar inputs have nearby vector representations. Vector databases are great in applications like semantic search and recommendation systems where we need to find items with similar meaning or features rather than exact matches. 


    This structure is optimised for similarity search over vector data rather than exact matching of text values. Unlike keyword-based search which relies on matching words, vector search retrieves results based on numerical similarity of embeddings. This allows it to overcome issues like synonyms or rephrasing. A good example: “raise an issue” and “create a ticket” would be represented by similar embeddings and thus can be matched by a vector search even though they share no keywords.


    Vector databases use specialised data structures to handle the high-dimensional vector data efficiently. Rather than scanning through every vector for each query (which would be very slow for large datasets), they employ approximate nearest neighbour (ANN) algorithms that accelerate similarity search. The most popular indexing strategy today is the so-called Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) graph algorithm. We will look into the other indexing algorithms later in the next section.



    Embedding Models and Their Outputs


    In this part of the lesson, we focus on the embedding part of the RAG ingestion pipeline.



    Embedding models are machine learning models (usually neural networks) that encode data into vector representations. For example, in NLP, a transformer model can produce a dense vector for a sentence, and that vector will be close to another sentence’s vector if the two sentences have similar meaning - even if they don’t share words. This property allows embeddings to naturally handle synonyms and even translate meaning across languages. There are many embedding models available, some are open-source models you can run yourself, others you can use through proprietary APIs:


    • OpenAI Embeddings: OpenAI provides API models for text embeddings, most famously the text-embedding-ada-002 model and its newer replacements in text-embedding-3 series. Depending on the model choice it generates 1536-dimensional or 1536-dimensional vectors for any input text. OpenAI’s embeddings are general-purpose and trained on very large corpora, which makes them perform well on a wide range of semantic tasks.

    • Sentence Transformers (Hugging Face models): There are many open-source embedding models, especially for text, which you can run on your own hardware. For example, the SentenceTransformers library provides pre-trained models like all-MiniLM-L6-v2 that produce 384-dimensional sentence embeddings. These models are based on Transformer architectures (BERT, RoBERTa, etc.) fine-tuned to generate meaningful vector representations for sentences or paragraphs. The power of open-source embedding models is that they can also be fine-tuned to specific domains if needed.

    • Other Providers: Aside from OpenAI, other AI providers offer embedding services - for example, Cohere and Google. The landscape of embedding models is rich and rapidly evolving.


    When choosing an embedding model, you should consider factors like the type of data (text vs image), the dimensionality of output, performance on your task (some models are better at capturing nuance in short text vs long documents, etc.), whether the model needs to be hosted by you or can be called via API, and cost constraints. Many pre-trained models are readily accessible on platforms like Hugging Face Hub, so you can experiment with different embeddings to see which works best for your application.


    Regardless of the specific model, the key idea is that you will use an embedding model to encode your data into vectors. These vectors then become the “keys” by which you index and retrieve information in a vector database. A crucial detail is that the embedding model used at query time must be the same (or compatible) as the one used on your indexed data – this ensures the query vector lives in the same semantic space as the stored vectors. (For example, you wouldn’t index documents with one model and then query with a completely different model’s embeddings, or else the results won’t make sense.).



    Vector Index Types and ANN Algorithms


    To achieve fast similarity search, vector databases rely on index structures and ANN algorithms. Different index types bring trade-offs between search accuracy, speed, and memory usage.



    Here are some common index types:


    • Flat (Brute-Force) Index: The simplest approach with no approximation - all vectors are stored as-is and a query is compared to every vector in the collection. This produces exact results (highest recall/accuracy) but is slow for large datasets because the search time grows linearly with the number of vectors. Flat indexes are feasible for small datasets or where absolute accuracy is paramount and latency is not an issue.

    • LSH (Locality-Sensitive Hashing): An ANN technique that hashes vectors into buckets such that similar vectors are likely to fall into the same bucket. LSH creates multiple random projections or hash functions to group vectors. When you perform the search, then you only compare vectors within the same hash buckets. It can be fast, but often requires many hash tables to reach high recall, and it struggles with very high-dimensional data (the required number of hash bits grows dramatically). LSH is generally best for lower-dimensional embeddings or smaller datasets and is less common in modern high-dimension use cases.

    • HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs): A state-of-the-art graph-based index. HNSW organises vectors in a multi-layer small-world graph where each vector (node) connects to its nearest neighbours. The search algorithm traverses this graph to find close items without examining all points. HNSW is known for excellent recall-quality at high speed. The trade-off: HNSW indexes can be memory-intensive, since each node stores multiple connections (the parameter M controls how many neighbours each point links to). Using higher M or search parameters increases recall but also increases memory consumption. In practice, most of the modern vector databases use HNSW.

    • IVF (Inverted File Index): This technique (pioneered in Facebook’s FAISS library) clusters the vector space into many clusters (Voronoi cells) and assigns each vector to its nearest cluster centroid. At query time, only the nearest n clusters to the query vector (controlled by a parameter like nprobe) are searched, rather than the entire dataset. This cuts down comparisons. IVF by itself can miss results if a nearest neighbour falls in a different cluster, but it achieves a good trade-off: high recall with much lower search latency and memory usage than brute force. Often IVF is combined with other techniques like quantisation to refine search.

    • PQ (Product Quantisation): A vector compression technique often used alongside indexes like IVF. PQ splits each vector into sub-components and quantises each sub-vector into a small code (using a codebook learned from data). The database then stores compact codes instead of full float vectors, greatly reducing memory. During search, distance is approximated by precomputed distances of codes. PQ allows very large collections to be searched with lower memory and faster disk reads, at the cost of some accuracy loss due to compression. Many systems offer IVF+PQ as a configuration (e.g. Milvus and FAISS) for an excellent speed/memory trade-off when a small loss in precision is acceptable.


    Each index type has scenarios where it’s preferred. E.g. HNSW is often a go-to for high-accuracy search on moderate hardware (RAM-heavy but fast), whereas IVF+PQ might be chosen to handle billion-scale vectors on disk.



    Distance Measures for Similarity


    When performing similarity search, we need a way to quantify how “close” two vectors are. The similarity metric is a crucial part of the system. The most commonly used distance measures in vector search are Euclidean distance, Cosine similarity, and Dot product (all of them are fully supported in Qdrant):


    • Cosine Similarity: Measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, effectively comparing their orientation rather than magnitude. Two vectors with the exact same orientation have cosine similarity 1.0 (maximum), orthogonal vectors - 0, diametrically opposed vectors have -1. Cosine similarity is popular in text embeddings because it focuses on the relative values in the vector. Often embeddings are normalized to unit length when using cosine similarity.


    • Dot Product: Computes the sum of element-wise products of two vectors. The dot product combines both direction and magnitude. If vectors are not normalized, a larger vector (e.g. from a longer document) can yield a higher dot product even if the direction is similar. In fact, if all vectors are normalized to length 1, the dot product is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity. Dot product is commonly used for embeddings where magnitude carries meaning.



    • Euclidean Distance (L2 distance): The L2 norm distance between two points in the vector space. It is calculated as the square root of the sum of squared differences between corresponding vector components. Euclidean distance treats the vector values as coordinates in space and measures absolute difference. Vectors that are very close in Euclidean terms are very similar. Euclidean is a natural choice when the magnitude of vector elements matters.



    There is no single “best” distance metric, it depends on your data and embedding model. Cosine is often preferred for textual semantic embeddings (where vector magnitudes may not be meaningful), while Euclidean or dot-product might be used in other cases or when vectors are already normalized by the model. The key is that the vector database will use the chosen metric to rank similarity: it will return vectors that minimize distance (or maximize similarity) to the query vector.


    I find it helpful to refer to the simplified 2D representation of the distance calculation for each of the metric. If you think of the angle of the vector as meaning representation and the magnitude (length) as something that enforces that specific meaning, the tradeoffs of each distance metric then becomes more apparent.
